title: Entanglement Generation During Distribution via Spatial Superposition Entanglement Generation
authors: Claudio Pellitteri, Rajiuddin Sk, Marcello Caleffi, Angela Sara Cacciapuoti
abstract: The exploitation of quantum coherence at the level of propagation represents a powerful paradigm for quantum communication networks. In this work, we show that the coherent superposition of spatially distinct communication links enables entanglement generation inherently during distribution. Specifically, separable quantum states can be deterministically transformed into entangled states, when the noisy communication links they traverse are coherently superposed. Contrary to the conventional view of noise as a detrimental effect, we demonstrate that quantum noise itself can be transformed into a constructive resource for entanglement generation for both bipartite and multipartite entanglement. Given the practical feasibility of implementing spatial superposition in interferometric setups, our approach provides a feasible method for distributed entanglement engineering, opening new directions for quantum communication and networked quantum technologies.
summary: This paper shows that by superposing different communication paths, one can use quantum noise to generate entanglement between previously separable states. It proves that Bell and GHZ states can be created deterministically even in highly noisy or entanglement-breaking regimes. This provides a new method for distributed entanglement engineering in quantum networks.
main_problem: The challenge of entanglement degradation due to noise during distribution in quantum networks and whether quantum noise can be harnessed as a resource for entanglement generation.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the coherent superposition of spatially distinct, noisy communication links can deterministically transform separable states into entangled Bell, GHZ, and W states, even in extreme noise regimes.
method: The study uses a mathematical framework of spatial superposition of channels, employing Kraus operators, vacuum extensions, and optimization of vacuum amplitudes to engineer interference patterns.
model_or_system: A quantum communication framework involving the coherent superposition of spatially distinct links (e.g., via interferometric setups like Sagnac or Mach-Zehnder) where a control system (qubit/qudit) governs the propagation of a target system through noisy channels.
key_observables: Fidelity (to Bell, GHZ, and W states) and Concurrence.
important_parameters: Noise probabilities (p, q) of depolarizing and bit/phase-flip channels, and vacuum amplitudes (alpha, beta) representing the physical configuration of the links.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that any relative phases introduced by different measurement outcomes or system sizes can be corrected via local unitary operations at the receiver.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a photonic setup for spatial superposition; Figure 2 and 3 illustrate the superposition of two and three links respectively; Figure 4 shows fidelity vs. noise probability; Figure 5 shows density plots of fidelity; Figure 6 shows concurrence vs. noise; Figure 7 and 8 show GHZ and W state generation via fidelity and concurrence.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the problem of noise-induced decoherence, proposes the spatial superposition framework, compares it to the Quantum Switch and Quantum Walks, details the deterministic generation of Bell, GHZ, and W states, and provides analytical proofs and numerical optimizations for various noise regimes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it proposes a paradigm shift where environmental noise, typically seen as a source of decoherence, is actively utilized as a constructive resource for entanglement engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Photonic setup for realizing the spatial superposition of two communication links, described by the quantum channel maps F(·) and N(·). The setup consists of two interferometers: a Sagnac interferometer for state preparation and a Mach- Zehnder interferometer for generating the superposition of the two links. The photon source enters the Sagnac interferometer, where two HWPs are rotated in a correlated manner; for a control qubit in the state |+⟩, both HWPs are oriented at 90◦with respect to the horizontal axis. Two compensators (each consisting of a HWP and a QWP) are placed at the two output ports of the Sagnac interferometer to correct path-dependent polarization effects. The two...
Fig. 2: Schematic diagram illustrating the superposition of two distinct quantum communication links, associated with the quantum channels F(·) and N(·).
Fig. 3: Schematic diagram illustrating the superposition of three links used to generate a 3-qubit W state. The control qubit is prepared in the state |e+⟩= 1 √

Fig. 4: Fidelity as a function of the noise probability (p = q) between the two-qubit output state obtained via the coherent superposition of the two channels and the Bell state |Φ+⟩. The different curves correspond to distinct choices of vacuum amplitudes, as indicated in the legend. Only specific configurations achieve unit fidelity, highlighting the critical role of the vacuum extension in enabling deterministic entanglement generation across the considered noise regimes.

Fig. 5: Density plots of the fidelity fid as a function of the noise probabilities p and q. Panels (a) and (b) correspond to the bipartite case, showing the fidelity with the Bell state |Φ+⟩for different vacuum configurations under the superposition of bit-flip and phase-flip channels. Panel (c) corresponds to the multipartite case, showing the fidelity with the GHZ state under the same channel superposition. As predicted by Cor. 1 and Cor. 2, appropriate choices of the vacuum amplitudes enable unit fidelity in the relevant operating regimes.
